title: Apply: motion
description: Apply your knowledge of motion in this set of free, standards-aligned practice questions.

assessment_items.question_content:
A wildlife biologist is studying wild cheetahs. She takes a video of a cheetah running at its top speed. 

[[☃ image 3]]

Then, she uses the video to make the graph of the cheetah’s distance vs. time shown below:

 [[☃ image 2]]

**What is the cheetah’s average speed during the $8\,\text{s}$ shown on the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Average speed is equal to distance divided by time:

$\text{speed}=\dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{time}}$

On a distance vs. time graph, this is the same as the slope of the graphed line:

$\text{slope}=\dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{time}}$
We need the cheetah’s average speed for the whole $8\,\text{s}$ shown. So, we need the distance the cheetah ran after $8\,\text{s}.$ We can get this from the graph:

[[☃ image 2]]

Now, let’s plug in the distance of $\color{red}{200\,\text{m}}$ and time of $\color{blue}8\,\text{s}$ to find the slope of the line, which is the cheetah’s average speed:

$\text{speed}=\dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{time}}$

$\text{speed}=\dfrac{\color{red}200\,\text{m}}{\color{blue}8\,\text{s}}$

$\text{speed}=25\,\dfrac{\color{red}{\text{m}}}{\color{blue}{\text{s}}}$
The cheetah’s average speed is $\boxed{25\,\dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}}.$

options.alt: A graph has distance in meters on the y-axis and time in seconds on the x-axis. There is a straight line graphed that goes from point (0, 0) to point (8, 200).
A cheetah is running across flat ground.

assessment_items.question_content:
When penguins swim, they occasionally jump up out of the water. This behavior, called *porpoising*, helps them swim faster because they can take breaths without slowing down. A wildlife biologist takes a video of a penguin swimming and porpoising.

[[☃ image 2]]

Then, she uses the video to make the graph of the penguin's distance vs. time shown below:

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the penguin's average speed during the $40\,\text{s}$ shown on the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Average speed is equal to distance divided by time:

$\text{speed}=\dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{time}}$

On a distance vs. time graph, this is the same as the slope of the graphed line:

$\text{slope}=\dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{time}}$
We need the penguin's average speed for the whole $40\,\text{s}$ shown. So, we need the distance the penguin swam after $40\,\text{s}.$ We can get this from the graph:

[[☃ image 5]]
Now, let’s plug in the distance of $\color{red}{100\,\text{m}}$ and time of $\color{blue}40\,\text{s}$ to find the slope of the line, which is the penguin's average speed:

$\text{speed}=\dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{time}}$

$\text{speed}=\dfrac{\color{red}100\,\text{m}}{\color{blue}40\,\text{s}}$

$\text{speed}=2.5\,\dfrac{\color{red}{\text{m}}}{\color{blue}{\text{s}}}$
The penguin's average speed is $\boxed{2.5\,\dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}}.$

options.alt: A graph has distance in meters on the y-axis and time in seconds on the x-axis. There is a straight line graphed that goes from point (0, 0) to point (40, 100).
Four penguins are jumping out of the water while swimming next to each other.

assessment_items.question_content:
The fastest known animal on Earth is the peregrine falcon. It flies very high then performs a *stoop* (a controlled dive) to catch its prey.

[[☃ image 6]]

Below is a graph of distance vs. time for a peregrine falcon during a stoop:

[[☃ image 5]]

**What is the falcon's average speed during the $12\,\text{s}$ shown on the graph?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Average speed is equal to distance divided by time:

$\text{speed}=\dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{time}}$

On a distance vs. time graph, this is the same as the slope of the graphed line:

$\text{slope}=\dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{time}}$
We need the falcon's average speed for the whole $12\,\text{s}$ shown. So, we need the distance the falcon dove after $12\,\text{s}.$ We can get this from the graph:

[[☃ image 5]]
Now, let’s plug in the distance of $\color{red}{900\,\text{m}}$ and time of $\color{blue}12\,\text{s}$ to find the slope of the line, which is the falcon's average speed:

$\text{speed}=\dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{time}}$

$\text{speed}=\dfrac{\color{red}900\,\text{m}}{\color{blue}12\,\text{s}}$

$\text{speed}=75\,\dfrac{\color{red}{\text{m}}}{\color{blue}{\text{s}}}$
The falcon's average speed is $\boxed{75\,\dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}}.$

options.alt: A graph has distance in meters on the y-axis and time in seconds on the x-axis. There is a straight line graphed that goes from point (0, 0) to point (12, 900).
A peregrine falcon's wings are partly tucked back as it flies at a steep downward angle during a dive.

assessment_items.question_content:
Students want to find the average speed of a car driving in front of their school. They choose a utility pole to mark a starting point, as shown below:

 [[☃ image 1]]

The students record how much time it takes the car to move from the starting point to each of the next four poles. Then they measure the distance from the start to each pole. The data table below shows their measurements:

 car's position| time to reach pole $(\text{s})$ | distance from start $(\text{m})$
:-: | :-: | :-:
starting pole | 0 | 0
pole 1 | 2.5 | 40
pole 2 | 5.0 | 80 
pole 3 | 7.5 | 120
pole 4 | 10.0 | 160

**What is the average speed of the car between the starting pole and pole 4?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}$
hints:
Average speed is equal to distance divided by time:

$\text{speed}=\dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{time}}$
We want the average speed from the starting pole to pole 4. So, we’ll use the distance from the start to pole 4 $(160\,\text{m})$ and the time to reach pole 4 $(10.0\,\text{s}).$

Let’s plug these numbers into the speed equation:

$\text{speed}=\dfrac{160\,\text{m}}{10.0\,\text{s}}$
When we divide these two numbers we get:

$\text{speed}=16\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}$

So, the car’s average speed is $\boxed{16\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}}.$

options.alt: A straight road with five utility poles spaced equally along it. The front edge of a car is just left of the first pole on the left, marked "start." There are motion lines showing that the car is moving. The following four poles are labeled, from left to right, 1, 2, 3, and 4.

assessment_items.question_content:
Students want to find the average speed of a car driving in front of their school. They choose a utility pole to mark a starting point, as shown below:

 [[☃ image 1]]

The students record how much time it takes the car to move from the starting point to each of the next four poles. Then they measure the distance from the start to each pole. The data table below shows their measurements:

 car's position| time to reach pole $(\text{s})$ | distance from start $(\text{m})$
:-: | :-: | :-:
starting pole | 0 | 0
pole 1 | 4 | 44
pole 2 | 8 | 88 
pole 3 | 12 | 132
pole 4 | 16 | 176

**What is the average speed of the car between the starting pole and pole 4?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}$
hints:
Average speed is equal to distance divided by time:

$\text{speed}=\dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{time}}$
We want the average speed from the starting pole to pole 4. So, we’ll use the distance from the start to pole 4 $(176\,\text{m})$ and the time to reach pole 4 $(16\,\text{s}).$

Let’s plug these numbers into the speed equation:

$\text{speed}=\dfrac{176\,\text{m}}{16\,\text{s}}$
When we divide these two numbers we get:

$\text{speed}=11\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}$

So, the car’s average speed is $\boxed{11\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}}.$

options.alt: A straight road with five utility poles spaced equally along it. The front edge of a car is just left of the first pole on the left, marked "start." There are motion lines showing that the car is moving. The following four poles are labeled, from left to right, 1, 2, 3, and 4.

Students want to find the average speed of a car driving in front of their school. They choose a utility pole to mark a starting point, as shown below:

 [[☃ image 1]]

The students record how much time it takes the car to move from the starting point to each of the next four poles. Then they measure the distance from the start to each pole. The data table below shows their measurements:

 car's position| time to reach pole $(\text{s})$ | distance from start $(\text{m})$
:-: | :-: | :-:
starting pole | 0 | 0
pole 1 | 5 | 60
pole 2 | 10 | 120 
pole 3 | 15 | 180
pole 4 | 20 | 240

**What is the average speed of the car between the starting pole and pole 4?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}$
hints:
Average speed is equal to distance divided by time:

$\text{speed}=\dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{time}}$
We want the average speed from the starting pole to pole 4. So, we’ll use the distance from the start to pole 4 $(240\,\text{m})$ and the time to reach pole 4 $(20\,\text{s}).$

Let’s plug these numbers into the speed equation:

$\text{speed}=\dfrac{240\,\text{m}}{20\,\text{s}}$
When we divide these two numbers we get:

$\text{speed}=12\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}$

So, the car’s average speed is $\boxed{12\,\tfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}}.$

assessment_items.question_content:
In a relay race, four runners (A, B, C, and D) work together to run a total distance of $400\,\text{m}.$ Each runner runs $100\,\text{m}$ before handing a baton to the next runner. 

A clock starts measuring time when runner A begins. The time is recorded when each runner completes their part of the race. These times are shown on the distance vs. time graph below.

 [[☃ image 2]]

**Which runner had the fastest average speed?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Average speed is equal to distance divided by time:

$\text{speed}=\dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{time}}$

The slope of a graphed line equals the change in the $y$-axis value divided by the change in the $x$-axis value.  On the graph above, the $y$-axis is distance and the $x$-axis is time. So, the slope equals distance divided by time:

$\text{slope}=\dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{time}}$
This is the same as the formula for speed.  So, the part of the graph with the greatest slope shows the greatest (fastest) average speed.  A greater slope looks *steeper.*
Runner D has the steepest slope. So, runner D had the fastest average speed.

options.alt: A graph has distance in meters on the y-axis and time in seconds on the x-axis. There are four straight line segments that are connected end-to-end. They are labeled, from left to right, A, B, C and D.  Segment "A" runs from point (0,0) to point (20,100).  Segment B runs from point (20,100) to point (60,200). It is less steep than segment A.  Segment C runs from point (60,200) to point (80,300). It is the same steepness as segment A. Segment D runs from point (80,300) to point (90,400). It is steeper than segments A and C.

assessment_items.question_content:
In a relay race, four runners (A, B, C, and D) work together to run a total distance of $400\,\text{m}.$ Each runner runs $100\,\text{m}$ before handing a baton to the next runner. 

A clock starts measuring time when runner A begins. The time is recorded when each runner completes their part of the race. These times are shown on the distance vs. time graph below.

 [[☃ image 2]]

**Which runner had the slowest average speed?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Average speed is equal to distance divided by time:

$\text{speed}=\dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{time}}$

The slope of a graphed line equals the change in the $y$-axis value divided by the change in the $x$-axis value.  On the graph above, the $y$-axis is distance and the $x$-axis is time. So, the slope equals distance divided by time:

$\text{slope}=\dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{time}}$
This is the same as the formula for speed.  So, the part of the graph with the smallest slope shows the smallest (slowest) average speed.  A smaller slope looks *less steep.*
Runner B has the least steep slope. So, runner B had the slowest average speed.

options.alt: A graph has distance in meters on the y-axis and time in seconds on the x-axis. There are four straight line segments that are connected end-to-end. They are labeled, from left to right, A, B, C and D.  Segment "A" runs from point (0,0) to point (20,100).  Segment B runs from point (20,100) to point (60,200). It is less steep than segment A.  Segment C runs from point (60,200) to point (80,300). It is the same steepness as segment A. Segment D runs from point (80,300) to point (90,400). It is steeper than segments A and C.

assessment_items.question_content:
In a relay race, four runners (A, B, C, and D) work together to run a total distance of $400\,\text{m}.$ Each runner runs $100\,\text{m}$ before handing a baton to the next runner. 

A clock starts measuring time when runner A begins. The time is recorded when each runner completes their part of the race. These times are shown on the distance vs. time graph below.

 [[☃ image 2]]

**Which runners had the same average speed?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Average speed is equal to distance divided by time:

$\text{speed}=\dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{time}}$

The slope of a graphed line equals the change in the $y$-axis value divided by the change in the $x$-axis value.  On the graph above, the $y$-axis is distance and the $x$-axis is time. So, the slope equals distance divided by time:

$\text{slope}=\dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{time}}$

This is the same as the formula for speed.  So, if two runners have the same slope, those runners had the same average speed.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
Runners A and C have the same slope.  So, runners A and C had the same average speed.

options.alt: A graph has distance in meters on the y-axis and time in seconds on the x-axis. There are four straight line segments that are connected end-to-end. They are labeled, from left to right, A, B, C and D.  Segment "A" runs from point (0,0) to point (20,100).  Segment B runs from point (20,100) to point (60,200). It is less steep than segment A.  Segment C runs from point (60,200) to point (80,300). It is the same steepness as segment A. Segment D runs from point (80,300) to point (90,400). It is steeper than segments A and C.

assessment_items.question_content:
A car is driving around a curved part of a road as shown below:

 [[☃ image 1]]

The dots in the diagram show the location of the car every $1$ second as it drives around the curve. They are equal distances apart.

**Consider the car’s motion when it’s at the position shown in the diagram. Complete the statement below.**

* The car [[☃ dropdown 1]] accelerating. This must be true because it is [[☃ dropdown 2]].
hints: Acceleration tells how quickly an object is changing its velocity. So to be accelerating, an object’s velocity has to be changing.

Velocity tells how fast an object is moving *and* the direction it is going.  If either one of these things are changing, then its velocity is changing.

The distance between each dot is equal. This means the car is going equal distances every second. So, its speed is not changing.

But it is going around a curve. So, its *direction* is changing.

The answer is:

The car is accelerating. This must be true because it is changing direction.

options.alt: A portion of road in the shape of a half-circle is shown from above. A car is about two-thirds of the way around the curve. There are equally-spaced dots behind the car showing the path it has traveled. A curved arrow points from the front of the car, showing that it is continuing around the curve.

assessment_items.question_content:
A car is driving around a curved part of a road as shown below:

 [[☃ image 1]]

The dots in the diagram show the location of the car every $1$ second as it drives around the curve. They are equal distances apart.

**Consider the car’s motion when it’s at the position shown in the diagram.  Which statement is true and why?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A portion of road in the shape of a half-circle is shown from above. A car is about two-thirds of the way around the curve. There are equally-spaced dots behind the car showing the path it has traveled. A curved arrow points from the front of the car, showing that it is continuing around the curve.

choices.content: The car is accelerating because it's speeding up.
The car is accelerating because it's changing direction.
The car is NOT accelerating because it's moving at a constant speed.
The car is NOT accelerating because objects can’t accelerate while turning.

assessment_items.question_content:
Cars A, B, and C are driving on a road as shown below:

 [[☃ image 1]]

The dots behind each car show their location every $1$ second as they drive.

**Select the true statement.**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Acceleration tells how quickly an object is changing its velocity. So to be accelerating, an object’s velocity has to be changing.

Velocity tells how fast an object is moving (its speed) *and* the direction it's moving.  If either one of these things are changing, then its velocity is changing.
The dots behind car A are getting farther apart as time goes on. This means car A's speed is increasing. So, car A is accelerating.
The dots behind car B are getting closer together. This means car B's speed is decreasing. A decreasing speed is a change in velocity. So, car B is accelerating.

The dots behind car C are equal distances apart. This means car C's speed is not changing. But it's moving around a curve. So, its *direction* is changing. A change in direction is a change in velocity. So, car C is accelerating.

The correct answer is:

All of the cars are accelerating.

options.alt:
An intersection of two roads is shown from above. The road that runs vertically in the picture has a car driving on each side. On the right side, starting at the bottom are five dots that are close together near the bottom, but get further apart as they go up. Near the top is a car labeled A, with an arrow pointing up. On the left side of the road are five more dots. Starting from the top, the dots are spaced far apart, but get closer together toward the bottom. A car labeled B is shown at the end of these 5 dots with a shorter arrow pointing down. 
A curved road approaches the intersection from the right. A car on this road is shown with a curved arrow pointing from the front of the car, around the curve, toward the intersection. Behind the car are equally-spaced dots along the curve.

choices.content: Only cars A and B are accelerating.
All of the cars are accelerating.
